interactions.summary: The student reads the violent Priam/Pyrrhus sequence: Priam prepares uselessly, Polites is hunted and dies before his parents, and Pyrrhus drags Priam through his son's blood before killing him at the altar.

Jiang repeatedly pauses the reading to identify the scene as a rewriting of the Iliad's Priam/Achilles reconciliation: Achilles' son replaces Achilles, Polites replaces Hector, and filial legacy is destroyed rather than healed.

speaker_notes.note: Alternation is classroom structure: student reads quoted poem, Jiang interrupts with interpretation and prompts to continue.

claims.claim: Pyrrhus' pursuit of Polites rewrites the Iliad: Polites becomes Hector, Pyrrhus replaces Achilles, and the death occurs under the eyes of Priam and Hecuba.
The Aeneid turns the Iliad's Priam-Achilles reconciliation into a scene where the son of Achilles destroys his father's moral legacy.
claims.temporal_scope: Dated interpretation of the quoted scene.

claims.claim: Jiang reads Priam's speech to Pyrrhus as accusing him of degrading Achilles because Achilles honored the suppliant Priam and returned Hector's body.
claims.temporal_scope: Dated interpretation tied to the quoted lines.

claims.claim: This section prepares Jiang's claim that Virgil negates the Iliad's lesson that love and forgiveness can come into the world even after war.
claims.temporal_scope: Dated literary claim in this lecture.

signature_moments.moment: The Iliad's emotional climax is rewritten as generational revenge: Achilles is dead, and his son comes to murder Priam.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Achilles is dead, and now his son is seeking to kill Priam
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shows the anti-Homer mechanism at work inside a single inherited scene.

signature_moments.moment: Polites becomes Hector so that the earlier world of heroic grief can be replayed as Roman horror.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Jiang reads the Aeneid as a machine for replacing Homeric reconciliation with spectacle and revenge.

signature_moments.moment: Pyrrhus kills Priam through the blood of Priam's son, turning family, altar, and burial honor into one desecrated scene.
signature_moments.source_phrase: slithering on through slicks of his son's blood
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The image carries the lecture's claim that Virgil makes violence morally pornographic.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Achilles' son, used by Virgil to invert Achilles' reconciliation with Priam into murder.
Jiang's reading of Polites' death as a replay of Hector's death before Priam and Hecuba.
The moral right Achilles honored in Homer and Pyrrhus violates in the Aeneid scene.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR says "Prime Hecuba" where the intended reference is Priam and Hecuba; the interpretive structure is clear.
